title: Experimental observation of three-dimensional Anderson localization of electromagnetic waves
authors: Antton Goïcoechea, Alexey Yamilov, Clément Ferise, Sergey E. Skipetrov, Hui Cao, Matthieu Davy
abstract: A prominent phenomenon in contemporary condensed matter physics is Anderson localization -- suppression of wave propagation in disordered systems as a result of interference effects. Despite being observed with various types of waves over the years, all prior attempts to reach Anderson localization of light in three-dimensional systems have been hampered by experimental artifacts. Here, we report an unambiguous experimental proof of three-dimensional Anderson localization of microwaves in disordered metal aggregates. By studying samples with different metal volume fractions, we show a clear difference between diffusive and localized behaviors, and the latter is confirmed by a scaling analysis of transmitted beam width in excellent agreement with theoretical and numerical results. Our demonstration opens avenues for both fundamental studies and practical applications of this extraordinary phenomenon.
summary: This paper experimentally demonstrates three-dimensional Anderson localization of microwaves in a disordered metal aggregate. By measuring the time-dependent transverse spreading of the wave beam, the authors distinguish between normal diffusive transport and localization, which is marked by the saturation of the beam width. This provides a robust, measurable confirmation of a key concept in condensed matter physics.
main_problem: The paper addresses the experimental observation of three-dimensional (3D) Anderson localization of electromagnetic waves, a phenomenon predicted to occur in disordered media.
main_result: The authors report an unambiguous experimental proof of 3D Anderson localization by observing the temporal arrest of transverse wave spreading in a highly disordered microwave medium.
method: The study combines microwave measurements using narrowband filters with theoretical analysis based on the Self-Consistent Theory (SCT) of localization.
model_or_system: The system is a disordered medium composed of randomly packed aluminum particles (metallic scatterers) through which microwaves propagate. The disorder is controlled by the metal volume fraction ($\phi$).
key_observables: Transverse spreading width squared, $w^2(t)$, and the total transmission spectrum $T(f)$.
important_parameters: Metal filling fractions ($\phi=15\%$ for diffusive, $\phi=60\%$ for localized); localization length ($\xi$); sample thickness ($L$).
main_assumptions: The measurements assume the ability to exclude broadband inelastic and nonlinear signals, and the analysis relies on the SCT framework.
figures_summary: Figures show comparisons of transmission spectra and the time evolution of the beam width ($w^2(t)$) for both diffusive and localized regimes, confirming saturation in the localized case.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical background (Anderson localization in 3D), details the experimental setup using microwave transmission, presents diagnostics by comparing time-dependent beam spreading in different disorder regimes, and validates the results using SCT and numerical simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a direct, measurable realization of wave localization in a solid-state system, offering an experimental platform to test theories of wave interference and transport in highly disordered media, which has parallels to quantum wave function localization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
